summary: Gruber responds to a Harvard Business Review article arguing Apple's record earnings aren't impressive because the company has stopped innovating. He counters that Apple has never been a serial revolutionary — its true revolutions (Apple II, Mac, iPod, iPhone) were few and far between, with constant iteration in between. He argues the real significance of Apple's quarter is that it proves design and quality can drive mass-market demand, upending decades of industry wisdom that design didn't matter. He contends Apple is actually disrupting business theory itself, as conventional disruption theory fails to explain a company whose premium products keep gaining market share.
key_points: Apple's history shows revolutionary products were rare; most progress came through constant iteration and refinement
Apple's record quarter proves that design and quality matter in the mass market, overturning decades of industry conventional wisdom
Average iPhone selling price went up year-over-year in an industry where prices trend downward — the most telling metric
Apple is disrupting disruption theory itself: Christensen's framework doesn't apply to consumer markets where design and integration drive demand
Critics like Sampere are trying to force existing business theory onto Apple rather than adjusting their theories to explain Apple's success
money_quotes: There's no reason to buy an iPhone 6 or 6 Plus other than because you're willing to pay a premium for superior hardware and software quality. And last quarter 74 million people around the world did just that.
The most telling — dare I say dazzling — number Apple revealed last week wasn't the number of iPhones they sold during the quarter, but the price people paid for them.
Average selling price went up year-over-year, in an industry where average selling prices are going down.

The problem isn't that Apple has changed. The problem is that Apple has not changed, and their continuing success is proving that conventional disruption theory does not apply to consumer-driven markets.
Apple is disrupting the conventional tenets of business even more than they are any particular product category in consumer electronics.
Rather than study it, understand it, describe it, and teach it, Sampere has chosen to deny that it's happening.
Apple's success has upended the industry's value system. Almost all of Apple's competitors value design more today than they did a decade ago.
key_insight: Apple's record quarter isn't remarkable because the iPhone 6 is revolutionary, but because it proves that design excellence can command premium pricing in a mass market — disrupting business theory more than any product category.
